title: Low-carbon heating options: what is available and when each makes sense
subtitle: Heat pumps, electric direct, biomass, and district heating compared
excerpt: Gas boilers are being phased out of new-build homes in England from 2025. For existing homes, the main replacement routes are air-source heat pumps, ground-source heat pumps, direct electric heating, biomass boilers, and district heating connections. Each has a different cost, carbon impact, and suitability profile.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Future Homes Standard consultation (2024). Energy Saving Trust heat pump and heating comparison guidance. Heat Pump Association market data (2025). Carbon Trust and BRE guidance on building heat decarbonisation pathways.

content.text: Heating uses about 80% of the energy in a UK home. Around 80% of that heat comes from natural gas. The carbon hit per kWh of grid electricity has fallen a long way (from 450 g/kWh in 2012 to about 150 g/kWh now), so electric heating gets cleaner each year. A
heat pump
running at
SCOP
3.0 produces approximately 50 g CO2e/kWh of heat, compared with a gas boiler at approximately 210 g CO2e/kWh.
Air-source heat pump (ASHP)
An ASHP extracts heat from outdoor air even at temperatures down to -15°C. SCOP for UK conditions is typically 2.5-3.5 depending on flow temperature and local climate.
BUS

grant deducted at point of sale by the installer. ASHPs are suitable for well-insulated homes with radiators or underfloor heating sized for low flow temperatures (35-45°C). Noise output from the outdoor unit: 40-50 dB at 1 m distance.
Ground-source heat pump (GSHP)

A GSHP extracts heat from the ground via buried pipework (horizontal slinky loops or vertical boreholes). Ground temperature in the UK stays at 8-12°C year-round, giving GSHPs a higher and more consistent SCOP (3.5-5.0) than ASHPs. Installation costs are higher than an ASHP due to groundwork. BUS grant applies. Suitable where garden space allows horizontal coils (approximately 2-3 times the floor area of the house) or where boreholes are acceptable (approximately 100 m deep per 5 kW). Silent operation.
Direct electric heating

Storage heaters (charged overnight on Economy 7), panel heaters, and infrared panels run on grid electricity. Carbon hit per kWh: about 150 g CO2e at today's grid mix. That is better than gas but worse than a heat pump. The running cost on a standard tariff (27p/kWh) is much higher than gas or a good heat pump. Storage heaters on Economy 7 (about 12p/kWh overnight) hold their own against gas in small, well-insulated homes. They suit flats, rentals, and very small houses, or any place where a heat pump is not on the table.
Biomass boiler

A biomass boiler burns wood pellets or wood chips and connects to the existing wet heating system. Pellet fuel cost is broadly comparable with mains gas per kWh. Carbon: counted as near-zero in UK policy but depends on fuel sourcing (UK-grown sustainable woodland vs imported pellets with high logistics carbon). BUS grant applies for eligible properties. Requires a fuel delivery and storage space. Suitable for rural properties with space for fuel delivery and with no gas connection.
District heating

In some areas there are district heating networks (mostly new city builds and social housing). Homes there get hot water from a central heat plant (usually a big heat pump, a gas-fired CHP, or waste heat from a factory). The building has a heat interface unit (HIU) in place of a boiler. The carbon cost depends entirely on what feeds the network. A home on a district network has no upgrade path of its own. The network's fuel mix sets the carbon footprint.
When each option makes sense
Detached house, 90+ m2, EPC C or better, garden: ASHP is the standard recommendation. Upgrade radiators in 2-3 rooms if needed. Apply for BUS grant.
Large home with a big garden and good insulation: a GSHP runs more cheaply and is silent. The up-front cost is higher.
Flat or property with no outdoor space for a unit: direct electric with Economy 7 storage heaters, or wait for the district network.
Rural home with no gas: a biomass boiler if you can take fuel deliveries, or an ASHP if the house is well insulated.
An
MCS

-certified heat pump installer will carry out a heat loss calculation (to BS EN 12831) and size the system. The heat loss calculation is the critical step: an undersized heat pump will not maintain temperature on the coldest UK design day.
